repos.description: An R package providing a modular, extendable system for checking research outputs for best practices.
repos.summary: Recent development activity for the 'scienceverse/metacheck' GitHub repository has focused on bug fixes, code refinement, and feature enhancements. Specifically, commits have addressed issues with validation, loops, and code checks, while also adding new features such as a sanitizing step and support for Zenodo repositories. The overall direction of development appears to be aimed at stabilizing and improving the functionality of the metacheck tool, with multiple contributors working to resolve bugs and implement new features. Additionally, there have been efforts to restructure internal functions and improve report validation, indicating a push towards enhancing the tool's overall performance and usability.

r_meta.description_text: A modular, extendable system for checking research outputs for best practices using text search, R code, and/or (optional) LLM queries.

repos.readme_excerpt:
![Made in Europe](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made_in_Europe-003399?logo=european-union&logoColor=FFCC00)






The goal of metacheck is to automatically check research outputs for best practices. You can find out more at <https://scienceverse.github.io/metacheck/>.


You can install the development version of metacheck from [GitHub](https://github.com/) with:

``` r
remotes::install_github("scienceverse/metacheck")
```



## Notes for Developers

You may not contribute any code unless you also contribute a test of this code.

Check tests/testthat/helper.R for custom test skip functions. All

recent_commits.author: Lakens

files.patch:
@@ -215,7 +215,7 @@ reg_check <- function(paper,
 
   ## guidance ----
   guidance <- c(
-    "RegCheck was developed by Jamie Cummins (<https://github.com/JamieCummins/regcheck>); the local Ollama server bundled in metacheck is inspired by the Ollama integration work of Matilda Gustafsson (<https://github.com/Matilda03>).",
+    "RegCheck was developed by Jamie Cummins (<https://github.com/JamieCummins/regcheck>); the local Ollama server bundled in metacheck is inspired by the Ollama integratio

@@ -176,7 +176,7 @@ without fetching it twice.
 
 The same comparison can run entirely on your own machine. Metacheck bundles
 a minimal local RegCheck server (inspired by the Ollama integration work by
-[Matilda Gustafsson](https://github.com/Matilda03)) together with
+[Matilda Fogato](https://github.com/Matilda03/regcheck-fork)) together with
 [Ollama](https://ollama.com). No text leaves your machine, which makes it
 suitable for confidential work and GDPR-sensitive contexts.
 
@@ -225,7 +225,

recent_commits.diff_summary: This commit, 'name fix', in the 'scienceverse/metacheck' repository updates the attribution text in the `reg_check.R` file and the `regcheck.Rmd` vignette to correct the name "Ollama" which seems to be a mistaken or outdated reference.

releases.name: v1.6.0
releases.body:
## What's Changed
* use pdftools instead of separate program Xpdf for pdf to text conversions
* make unit tests for file-to-text tests more resilient by using a spreadsheet with manually coded references (instead of hard coding them in the scripts)

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/Michele

r_meta.description_text: A "spellchecker" for statistics. It checks whether your p-values match their accompanying test statistic and degrees of freedom. statcheck searches for null-hypothesis significance test (NHST) in APA style (e.g., t(28) = 2.2, p < .05). It recalculates the p-value using the reported test statistic and degrees of freedom. If the reported and computed p-values don't match, statcheck will flag the res

repos.readme_excerpt:
`statcheck` is a “spellchecker” for statistics. It checks whether your
*p*-values match their accompanying test statistic and degrees of
freedom.

`statcheck` searches for null-hypothesis significance test (NHST) in APA
style (e.g., *t*(28) = 2.2, *p* \< .05). It recalculates the p-value
using the reported test statistic and degrees of freedom. If the
reported and computed p-values don’t match, `statcheck` will flag the
result as an error.

![](man/figures/infograph.png)


`statcheck` is mainly useful for:

1.  **Self-checks**: you can use `statcheck` to make sure your
    manuscript doesn’t c

### Summary of v0.4.0 (previous major release)
- Added support for acknowledged contributors and unnamed contributors.
- Introduced separate contributor statements for authors and acknowledgees.
- Enhanced JATS 1.3 XML output with acknowledgees, funding, and conflict of interest statements.
- Ad

releases.name: Support for Acknowledged Contributors and ORCID
releases.body:
- Support for acknowledged contributors and those who do not want to be named
- Separate contributor statements for acknowledgees and authors
- Enhanced JATS 1.3 XML output with acknowledgees, funding, and conflict of interest statements
- ORCID badge integration with hyperlink or plain text opti

releases.name: 🚀 Custom Validation & New Outputs
releases.body:
Compared to the stable v0.1.0 release, this version introduces the following changes:

    ✅ New Output Options: Generate outputs for funding information and conflict of interest statements.
    🔍 Custom Validation: Enhanced input validation at the output level for more reliable data handling.

releases.name: Last version before deluxe column enhancement
releases.body: We are about to add more affiliation columns and a new validation system, so we are making this release in case anyone relies on the existing version.

releases.name: tenzing: documening contributorship with CRediT
releases.body: Tenzing is an easy-to-use web-based app and R package, that allows researchers to generate reports about the contribution of each team member on a project using CRediT, for insertion into their manuscripts and for publishers to potentially incorporate into article metadata.

repos.readme_excerpt:
[![Lifecycle:
experimental](https://img.shields.io/badge/lifecycle-experimental-orange.svg)](https://www.tidyverse.org/lifecycle/

Tenzing, an easy-to-use web-based app, allows researchers to generate
reports about the contribution of each team member on a project using
CRediT, for insertion into their manuscripts and for publishers to
potentially incorporate into article metadata.

[CRediT](http://credit.niso.org/) (Contributor Roles Taxonomy) is a
taxonomy of 14 categories of contributions to scientific scholarly
output. Each researcher can indicate which category they contributed to
in a sc

releases.name: scrutiny 0.5.0
releases.body:
The package is now released under the MIT license.

## Breaking changes

-   The `ratio` column in the output of `grim_map()` and `grim_map_seq()` was replaced by a `probability` column. This means:

    -   Numerically, the only difference is that `probability` is zero whenever `ratio` was negative

releases.name: scrutiny 0.4.0
releases.body:
This version brings major performance improvements. Furthermore:

## Bugfixes

-   Fixed a bug in `audit_seq()`: If the `dispersion` argument in the preceding call to a function like `grim_map_seq()` was specified as something other than a linearly increasing sequence, the `"diff_*"` columns in the

releases.name: scrutiny 0.3.0
releases.body:
## Duplicate analysis overhaul

The `duplicate_*()` functions now present their output better and have overall been streamlined. Read more at `vignette("duplicates")`.

-   A new function, `duplicate_tally()`, marks each observation with its overall frequency. It is similar to `duplicate_detect()` b

# scrutiny 0.2.3

Some new features and bugfixes:

-   New `audit()` methods for the output of `audit_seq()` and `audit_total_n()`.

-   New `duplicate_count_colpair()` function that checks each combination of columns in a data frame for duplicates.

-   New `restore_zeros_df()` function to

r_meta.title: Error Detection in Science
r_meta.authors: c( person(given = "Lukas", family = "Jung", role = c("aut", "cre"), email = "jung-lukas@gmx.net"), person(given = "Aurélien", family = "Allard", role = c("ctb")), person(given = "Nicolas Roman", family = "Posner", role = c("ctb")) )
r_meta.description_text: Test published summary statistics for consistency (Brown and Heathers, 2017, <doi:10.1177/1948550616673876>; Allard, 2018, <https://aurelienallard.netlify.app/post/anaytic-grimmer-possibility-standard-deviations/>; Heathers and Brown, 2019, <https://osf.io/5vb3u/>). The package also provides infrastructure for implementing new error detection techniques.

repos.readme_excerpt:
[![Codecov test
coverage](https://codecov.io/gh/lhdjung/scrutiny/branch/main/graph/badge.svg)](https://app.codecov.io/gh/lhdjung/scrutiny?branch=main)




The goal of scrutiny is to test published summary statistics for
consistency using techniques like GRIM and to check their plausibility.
The package makes these methods easy to use in a tidyverse-friendly way.
It hopes to help the new field of error detection go mainstream.

You can use the most important parts in the [Error
detection](https://errors.shinyapps.io/scrutiny) Shiny app instead.

Besides ready-made tests, scrutiny features a com

It is no longer sufficient for a citation or accesion number, or link to be in the same sentence as "was_available".
PDF to text conversion was improved to remove left margin text and deal

r_meta.authors: c(person("Nico", "Riedel", role = "aut"), person("Vladislav", "Nachev", role = c("aut", "cre"), email = "vladislav.nachev@charite.de", comment = c(ORCID = "0000-0003-0521-6153")))
r_meta.description_text: Detection of Open Data & Open Code statements in biomedical publications. Uses publication PDFs as input and returns both Open Data/Code: 'TRUE/FALSE', the detected statement, as well as additional metadata.

repos.readme_excerpt:
ODDPub is a text mining algorithm that parses a set of publications and detects
which publications disseminated Open Data or Open Code together with the
publication. It is tailored towards biomedical literature.


Nico Riedel, Vladislav Nachev (vladislav.nachev@charite.de), Miriam Kip, Evgeny Bobrov (evgeny.bobrov@bih-charite.de) - QUEST Center for Transforming Biomedical Research, Berlin Institute of Health


More information on the development and validation of the algorithm can be found in the publication https://doi.org/10.5334/dsj-2020-042. The related training and validation datasets can

repos.description: Workflow for Open Reproducible Code in Science: R package and RStudio project template.

r_meta.description_text: Create reproducible and transparent research projects in 'R'. This package is based on the Workflow for Open Reproducible Code in Science (WORCS), a step-by-step procedure based on best practices for Open Science. It includes an 'RStudio' project template, several convenience functions, and all dependencies required to make your project reproducible and transparent. WORCS is explained in the tutor

r_meta.title: Identifies indicators of transparency
r_meta.authors: c(person("Stylianos", "Serghiou", role = c("aut", "cre"), email = "stelios.serghiou@gmail.com"), person("Nachev", "Vladislav", role = c("aut"), email = "vladislav.nachev@charite.de") )
r_meta.description_text: Use this package to identify indicators of transparency within the published literature. It can identify and extract text related to indicators of transparency from specifically formatted TXT files and from PMC XML files (i.e. XML files downloaed from the PubMed Central).

repos.readme_excerpt:
<div align="justify">
  

`rtransparent` is an R package that automatically identifies and extracts indicators of transparency (data availability, code availability, statements of conflicts of interest, statements of funding, statements of protocol registration) from TXT and PMC XML versions of published research articles in biomedicine.



Stylianos (Stelios) Serghiou (sstelios@stanford.edu). This package also utilizes functions from the `oddpub` package of Nico Riedel (nico.riedel@bihealth.de).


## Publication

`rtransparent` was validated and subsequently used to extract indicators of tran

repos.summary: Recent development activity for the GitHub repository 'Davidvandijcke/coarse' has focused on refining the web interface, models, and pipeline functionality. Specifically, updates have included adding new models such as Claude Fable 5, recalibrating cost estimates for reasoning models, and improving the web interface with features like per-comment Discuss chat streaming and defaulting to latest host models. Additionally, several bug fixes have been implemented to address issues with OpenRouter key handling, extraction output, and security vulnerabilities, indicating an overall direction of development towards enhancing user experience, model support, and reliability.

releases.name: v1.1.3 — coarse-ink PyPI rename (fixes #17)
releases.body:
## What's new

First release under the new `coarse-ink` PyPI name. `uvx coarse-ink review paper.pdf ...` now works end-to-end.

### Fixed

- **PyPI distribution renamed to `coarse-ink`** — the bare `coarse` name on PyPI was already taken by an unrelated, abandoned package (`coarse==0.0.1`), so `uvx

releases.name: v1.1.0
releases.body:
## What's new

### Added
- **Multi-format support** — review .txt, .md, .tex, .docx, .html, and .epub files in addition to PDF. Docling (if installed) for high-quality conversion, with lightweight fallbacks. New optional dependency: `pip install coarse[formats]`.
- **No-retention provider routing**

repos.readme_excerpt:
Free, open-source AI academic paper reviewer that outperforms popular paid AI reviewers.

You provide your own API key and pay the LLM provider directly — typically **under $2 per review**.

Don't want to run it locally? Use the [web interface](https://coarse.vercel.app/) instead.


Get an API key from [OpenRouter](https://openrouter.ai/keys) (free to sign up), then:

```bash
uvx coarse-ink review paper.pdf --api-key sk-or-v1-YOUR_KEY
```

That's it. The review is written to `paper_review.md` in the current directory.


coarse requires Python 3.12+. If you don't have `uvx`, install [uv](https:

repos.description: Tools for checking registered reports and pre-registrations against published results.
repos.summary: Recent development activity for the 'JamieCummins/regcheck' GitHub repository has focused on enhancing the user interface, improving report functionality, and adding API support. The commits indicate a significant overhaul of the report page, including redesigns, aesthetic updates, and improvements to report navigation, editing, and security. Additionally, the repository has seen updates to the API configuration, the addition of OAuth implementation, and various bug fixes, suggesting a direction towards a more robust, user-friendly, and secure application. Overall, the development activity suggests a major refinement of the repository's core features and user experience.

releases.name: v0.2.0-beta — Initial Public Release
releases.body:
This is the first open-source beta release of RegCheck: a FastAPI web app and CLI for AI-assisted comparison of preregistrations/clinical trial registrations/preclinical registrations against published papers. 
The project is under active development and evaluation and the interface, outputs, and d

repos.readme_excerpt:
AI-assisted comparison tool for preregistrations/clinical trial registrations/preclinical (animals) registrations and published papers. FastAPI serves the web UI and HTTP API; a CLI entrypoint enables backend-only runs with CSV-defined dimensions. Redis is used for task state when running via the web app.

Status: beta (under active development).

- `app.py` / `backend/`: FastAPI app, routes, services (comparisons, embeddings, parsing).
- `templates/` + `static/`: Frontend pages and assets.
- `uploads/`: Runtime uploads directory (created at runtime; ignored by git).
- `backend/worker.py`: Bac

r_meta.title: Statistical Consistency Checker for Published Research Results
r_meta.authors: c(person(given = "Gilad", family = "Feldman", role = c("aut", "cre"), email = "giladfel@gmail.com", comment = c(ORCID = "0000-0003-2812-6599")))
r_meta.description_text: A conservative, assumption-aware statistical consistency checker for already-extracted research-results text. Parses test statistics, effect sizes, and confidence intervals across multiple citation styles including American Psychological Association (APA), Harvard, Frontiers, PLOS ONE, Scientific Reports, Nature Human Behaviour, PeerJ, eLife, PNAS, and others. Recomputes effect sizes using all pla

repos.readme_excerpt:
> **IMPORTANT: Development Version.** This package is under heavy development
> and has not yet been fully validated. Results should be independently verified
> before use in any consequential context (e.g., peer review, retractions,
> editorial decisions). Use of this package is at the sole responsibility of
> the user. We welcome contributions, verification reports, and bug reports at
> <https://github.com/giladfeldman/escicheck/issues> or by contacting
> Gilad Feldman (<giladfel@gmail.com>).

**EffectCheck** is a conservative, assumption-aware statistical consistency checker for published r

recent_commits.author: Gilad Feldman

files.patch:
@@ -1,7 +1,7 @@
 Package: effectcheck
 Type: Package
 Title: Statistical Consistency Checker for Published Research Results
-Version: 0.6.1
+Version: 0.6.2
 Authors@R: c(person(given = "Gilad", family = "Feldman", role = c("aut", "cre"),
     email = "giladfel@gmail.com", comment = c(ORCID = "0000-0003-2812-6599")))
 Description: A conservative, assumption-aware statistical consistency checker

@@ -1,3 +1,30 @@
+# effectcheck 0.6.2
+
+Exact binomial test reported with Cohen's h. New `test_type = "binomial"`
+matched via `pat_binom_h`, anchored on a "binomial p [op] <pval>" clause
+followed (within ~80 non-period chars) by "Cohen('s)? h = <h>". When a
+"<n> out of <N>" clause is present in the same verbatim, N is recovered
+(`N_source = "binom_n_out_of_N"`) and check.R re-computes the two-sided
+binomial p via `stats::binom.test()` assuming p_null = 0.5 (the most
+common null in binomia

@@ -2922,6 +2922,51 @@ compute_and_compare_one <- function(row,
       paste("Cochran Q reported as a meta-analytic heterogeneity test -",
             "no standard effect size; consistency check is on the reported",
             "p-value vs chi-square(Q, df). I-squared not independently verified."))
+  } else if (tt == "binomial") {
+    # ------ EXACT BINOMIAL TEST WITH COHEN'S H ------
+    # v0.6.2: When the verbatim is "<n> out of <N> ... binomial p [op] X,
+    # Cohen's h = Y", the parser

@@ -726,6 +726,26 @@ parse_text <- function(text, context_window_size = 2) {
   #   homogeneity null with the reported df). The "_<letter>" subscript is
   #   optional; the df may be in [brackets] or (parens) with optional spaces.
   pat_cochran_q <- "\\bQ(?:_[A-Za-z])?\\s*[\\[(]\\s*(\\d+(?:\\.\\d+)?)\\s*[\\])]\\s*=\\s*([-+]?\\d*\\.?\\d+)"
+  # v0.6.2: exact binomial test with Cohen's h effect size.
+  # Form: "(exact )?binomial p [op] <pval>[, ]Cohen('s)? h = <h>[, 95% CI [<lo>, <hi>]]"
+  # T

@@ -7,7 +7,7 @@
 check_text(
   text,
   stats = c("t", "F", "r", "chisq", "z", "U", "W", "H", "regression", "spearman",
-    "kendall", "kendall_w", "dscf", "cochran_q", "RR", "rdpct", "md_hl"),
+    "kendall", "kendall_w", "dscf", "cochran_q", "RR", "rdpct", "md_hl", "binomial"),
   ci_level = 0.95,
   alpha = 0.05,
   one_tailed = FALSE,

@@ -0,0 +1,68 @@
+# v0.6.2: exact binomial test reported with Cohen's h
+#
+# Form: "<n> out of <N> ... binomial p [op] X, Cohen's h = Y, [95% CI [...]]"
+# Common in decoy-effect / binary-choice replication papers (CRSP, JESP).
+#
+# Before v0.6.2 these returned 0 rows. v0.6.2 adds pat_binom_h with two
+# anchors (binomial p AND Cohen's h within ~80 non-period chars) plus a
+# NOTE-only check.R branch that recomputes the binomial p via binom.test()
+# when n / N are recoverable from a "<n> out

r_meta.title: A Metadata and Text Extraction and Manipulation Tool Set
r_meta.authors: c(person("Ingmar", "Böschen", email = "ingmar.boeschen@uni-hamburg.de", role = c("aut", "cre"), comment = c(ORCID = "0000-0003-1159-3991")))
r_meta.description_text: Provides a function collection to extract metadata, sectioned text and study characteristics from scientific articles in 'NISO-JATS' format. Articles in PDF format can be converted to 'NISO-JATS' with the 'Content ExtRactor and MINEr' ('CERMINE', <https://github.com/CeON/CERMINE>). For convenience, two functions bundle the extraction heuristics: JATSdecoder() converts 'NISO-JATS'-tagged XML files

repos.readme_excerpt:
A metadata and text extraction and text manipulation tool set for the statistical programming language [R](www.r-project.org). 

**JATSdecoder** facilitates text mining projects on scientific articles by enabling an individual selection of metadata and text parts. 
Its function `JATSdecoder()` extracts metadata, sectioned text and reference list from [NISO-JATS](https://jats.nlm.nih.gov/publishing/tag-library/1.1d2/index.html) coded XML files. 
The function `study.character()` uses the `JATSdecoder()` result to perform fine-tuned text extraction tasks to identify key study characteristics like

repos.description: Automated z-curve analysis for estimating replicability and discovery rates in sets of studies.
repos.summary: The GitHub repository 'shaheedazaad/auto-zcurve' has seen recent development activity focused on refining its documentation. Specifically, the repository's README file was updated multiple times on May 26, 2026, suggesting improvements to the project's description or usage instructions. More recently, on June 14, 2026, a validation note was revised and a citation was added, indicating an effort to enhance the project's credibility and academic rigor. Overall, the development direction appears to be centered on polishing the project's presentation and legitimacy.
repos.readme_excerpt:
Auto Z-Curve reads a folder of PDF articles, uses Google's Gemini AI to extract focal statistical results from each one, and produces a z-curve analysis report — including a plot, summary statistics, and a full disclosure table.

You interact with it through a simple terminal interface: point it at your PDFs, enter your API key, and click Run.

In the below screenshot, I z-curved 45 articles on cognitive dissonance in under 4 minutes, costing under 2 USD in tokens.

![](assets/example.svg)

**Note:** I have validated statistic extraction as part of a study, which is currently under peer review

recent_commits.message: Revise validation note and include citation
recent_commits.author: Shaheed Azaad

files.patch:
@@ -8,7 +8,9 @@ In the below screenshot, I z-curved 45 articles on cognitive dissonance in under
 
 ![](assets/example.svg)
 
-**Note:** this tool has not yet been peer-reviewed, although a validation study is in the works.
+**Note:** I have validated statistic extraction as part of a study, which is currently under peer review. The preprint citation is below, and details of the validation are in the supplemental material.
+
+Azaad, S. (2026). Empirically derived effect size guidelines for socia

recent_commits.diff_summary: This commit revises the validation note in the README.md file of the 'auto-zcurve' repository, adding a citation and updating the language to reflect the current status of the tool's peer review.

digest.narrative: Our community has been buzzing with activity, particularly in the 'scienceverse/metacheck' repository, where developers have been hard at work refining the metacheck tool. With a focus on bug fixes, code refinement, and feature enhancements, the team has made significant progress in stabilizing and improving the tool's functionality. Notably, the addition of a sanitizing step and support for Zenodo repositories highlights the project's commitment to interoperability and data integrity. As multiple contributors collaborate to resolve bugs and implement new features, we're seeing exciting connections between metacheck and other projects, with potential applications in data validation and repository management. This progress is a testament to the power of open-source collaboration and we're eager to see the impact of these developments on the broader research community. With metacheck's enhanced performance and usability, we're poised to take a significant leap forward in our shared pursuit of scientific excellence.
